evidence_quotes.quote: The other problem with video games, which we are trying to fix, is that publishers often do not have any end-of-life plans for their video games.

paragraphs.text: Concerning addressing problematic features of digital products such as in video games, I wholeheartedly agree that minors should not be able to play some of the games, the same way that they should not watch some movies before they can fully comprehend and grasp some of the themes and concepts and can react to them in a rational manner. The other problem with video games, which we are trying to fix, is that publishers often do not have any end-of-life plans for their video games. They can just turn off their servers and a game, even it is single player one, will be practically dead for all players. Yes, we have a lot of examples when through ingenuity and passion gamers try to preserve games without any help from the publishers. Yes, some of the publishers support game preservation and do something about it, like for example 'GOG.com' does. But, as I'm sure other people in this feedback section have already written, hundreds of games are just dead. And all the worlds created by thousands of artists, designers, audio engineers and so on are lost to time. We live in the time, when we can preserve our art and the art of fellow human beings. Sometimes I play an online video game called 'The Division' and just marvel at all the work done by countless artists to create a realistic vision of snowy New York struck by an epidemic. As we have it now, when Ubisoft turns the servers off, all of this will be lost. We do not ask publishers and developers to maintain games for all of our lives, just to design them in a way so that after they can't make anymore money off them, we can still preserve our copies of the game and enjoy the worlds created by such talented artists in the future.
